---
title: 'Quantitative biology and integrative omics <span style="color:#c0392b">(under construction)</span>'
title-block-banner: false
---

A second-cycle course (**3MB00A**, 7.5 ECTS, Umeå University) on the mathematical
and statistical methods that underpin modern bioinformatics. The unit of design
is the **method ↔ biology pairing**: every mathematical idea is introduced
together with a biological problem that motivates it.

## Where to start

- [**Audience**](audience.md) — who this course is for, and what we can and cannot assume they already know.
- [**Course plan**](courseplan.md) — the official syllabus: outcomes, examination, entry requirements.
- [**Learning outcomes**](elo.md) — what we are trying to teach, day by day.

## Before you start

- [**Install R and the packages you need**](intro/install.qmd) — do this before day 1.

## Topics

The course covers the basic mathematical and statistical methods widely used in
bioinformatics:

- **Linear algebra** — matrices, vectors, eigenvalues; solving systems of linear equations.
- **Markov chains** — formulating biological problems as state transitions.
- **Dimensional reduction** — PCA and OLS; latent-space methods for large-scale data.
- **Statistical distributions** — the Poisson and negative binomial distributions and where they arise in count data.
- **Bayesian statistics** — Bayesian vs. frequentist approaches.
- **Applications** — chemometrics and single-cell analysis.

<!-- Day-by-day exercise links go here once the material is written. -->

## Working with the exercises

Each exercise pairs a short `.qmd` walkthrough with worked R code:

- The walkthrough holds the prose, the hints (as collapsible callouts), and a reference solution.
- Code chunks are written in R; students run them and adapt them to the exercise data.

## Building this site

```bash
make           # render the whole site to _site/
make preview   # start a live-reload preview server
make clean     # delete _site/ and the Quarto cache
```

You will need [Quarto](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/) and a recent
[R](https://cran.r-project.org/) installation.
